title: Qlog Baseline Architecture
purpose: Describe how the qlog v1 implementation can satisfy the draft main-schema and QUIC requirements through a contained JSON core, QUIC vocabulary slices, and a deferred sequential follow-on.
design_summary: Keep a single Incursa.Qlog assembly for v1, with layered namespaces rather than extra projects: `Incursa.Qlog` for the core file, trace, and event types; `Incursa.Qlog.Serialization.Json` for the contained JSON writer boundary; `Incursa.Qlog.Quic` for the typed QUIC vocabulary contracts; and `Incursa.Qlog.Adapters` for later producer-facing integration. The architecture keeps the qlog core model separate from QUIC-specific vocabulary, and it keeps sequential JSON Text Sequences as a later slice. V1 should stand up the contained JSON writer and the typed QUIC event contracts through small adapter layers so envelope state, event state, and transport state do not collapse into one object model.
key_components: contained file and trace envelope handling
data_and_state_considerations: The core model should preserve file-envelope state separately from trace state so contained qlog forms can be validated without binding the serializer to QUIC semantics. The QUIC adapters should map transport state into qlog events while preserving unknown fields, extension members, and draft-version drift so the baseline remains stable as the I-Ds evolve. Sequential output should not force the core model to carry sequential-specific behavior in v1.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Draft section numbers and event names may shift as the upstream drafts evolve.
Contained and sequential file forms use different envelope rules and should not be conflated.
Unknown common fields and extension payloads must survive round-tripping.
Sequential JSON Text Sequences stay outside the first implementation pass.
HTTP/3 and future protocol vocabularies stay outside the first baseline.
alternatives_considered: One merged qlog writer versus a slice-first contained writer with a deferred sequential follow-on.
One assembly with layered namespaces versus multiple projects for core, serialization, and QUIC vocabulary.
A generator-first workflow versus a first-pass hand-authored baseline backed by validation.
Immediate multi-protocol coverage versus a bounded QUIC-only first slice.
A single QUIC adapter versus family-based QUIC slices.
risks: Draft churn could invalidate section references, event names, or URI details.
Overly broad abstractions could hide transport details that diagnostics need to preserve.
Sink-specific work could pressure the core model before the qlog envelope stabilizes.
Sequential behavior could creep into v1 if it is not kept behind a follow-on boundary.
open_questions: Should non-JSON sinks wait until the core model and QUIC adapter stabilize?
Should sequential JSON Text Sequences remain a separate follow-on until the contained JSON writer is stable?
Should future protocol vocabularies share this architecture slice or get their own baseline artifacts?
